rulings.Case Title: DONALD NORMAN VS. PATRICK STRATEMAN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff Donald Normans Notice Of Motion And Motion To Reopen Discovery And For Further Deposition Of Patrick Strateman (Cal. Civ. Proc. Code Sections 2024.050, 2024.290)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Tuesday, August 18, 2020, Line 1, PLAINTIFF DONALD NORMAN'S Motion To Reopen Discovery And For Further Deposition Of Patrick Strateman (Cal. Civ. Proc. Code Sections 2024.050, 2024.290). Pro Tem Judge Bruce Highman, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion to reopen discovery is denied. Discovery was previously reopened to some extent. The court doesn't believe it should keep being reopened. Plaintiff has not been diligent about seeking to reopen discovery related to the database and also refused to answer questions at his last deposition in January that went beyond the subjects on which the court previously reopened discovery. This included without limitation refusing to answer questions related to the database. As a result of the COVID-19 emergency, all attorneys and parties are required to appear remotely. Hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to bruce.highman@highmanlaw.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If parties have arranged for a court reporter to appear, please include the following in your email: (1) ensure that they are California Certified Shorthand Reporters; (2) the reporter's full contact information [name, CSR number, personal work email, and phone number]. If the reporter is not a California Certified Shorthand Reporter, the transcript cannot be certified for use in California courts. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must e-mail it to the Judge Pro Tem. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: NIKKI BAKER VS. FCA US LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Payment Of Attorney'S Fees And Reimbursement Of Costs And Expenses
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, August 18, 2020, Line 2, PLAINTIFF NIKKI BAKER's Motion For Payment Of Attorney'S Fees And Reimbursement Of Costs And Expenses. Moving party, including in the Reply, generally explains and supports the hours expended in the case, and I find that the time spent is, generally, reasonable. The rates are reasonable, and no evidence is submitted by defendant's late opposition to suggests otherwise. The use of 10 attorneys is explained, as almost all the work was done by three lawyers. The defendant makes a few specific objections (Opposition at 7-8) but the objections generally do not fairly represent the time entries. In an argument not addressed by the reply, defendant objects to $36,354 spent on the present motion for fees, and I agree the time, incurred after plaintiff's counsel knew it would be entitled to fees, is grossly excessive. On the basis of my review of the motion I reduce the sums to $15,000, a reduction of $21,354. Because of the contingency fee, a multiplier of 0.1 is warranted; the other factors pertaining to multipliers I find are accounted for in the lodestar rate. It appears the lodestar sought is $84,558 (the Notice of motion does not state the amount), and thus the basic award is $63,204, and with a multiplier, that results in an award of $69,524.40. The costs are not contested and are awarded. The proposed order sent to the court for signature should also state the amount of costs. As a result of the COVID-19 emergency, all attorneys and parties are required to appear remotely. Hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which states that the motion was not opposed and must email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/CEK)

rulings.Case Title: MARITZA HERRERA VS. MICHAEL MCDERMOTT MD
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, August 18, 2020, Line 4, DEFENDANT THE REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA's DEMURRER to COMPLAINT. Demurer sustained with leave to amend, amended complaint must be filed not later than August 31, 2020. Plaintiffs are representing themselves, and are urged to secure a lawyer. They should correctly and completely fill out the judicial council forms used for the complaint. Plaintiffs must address the apparent one year statute of limitations: it may be that the case had to be filed within 1 year of April 2017, that is, by April 4, 2018. If there is some reason why that one year period does not apply, facts to support that should be in the next complaint. Facts must be stated as to why each defendant is responsible. As a result of the COVID-19 emergency, all attorneys and parties are required to appear remotely. Hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. rulings.Case Title: PMGI FINANCIAL LLC., A LIMITED LIABILITY COMPANY VS. ZORANA BREED ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, August 18, 2020, Line 7, DEFENDANT ZORANA BREED's MOTION FOR SET ASIDE/VACATE JUDGMENT. Defendant's motion to set aside the default judgment is granted. The court notes that the motion does not appear to address the setting aside of the default. (The papers generally refer to the "default" without differentiating but the captions on the papers say "motion to vacate default judgment," although the notice states that Defendant is moving "for an order to set aside the default issued in the case.") See e.g., Weil & Brown, et al., California Practice Guide: Civil Procedure Before Trial 5:403, 5:413 (Rutter: 2020). CCP section 473(d) provides that the court "may, on motion of either party . . . set aside any void judgment or order." "[C]ompliance with the statutory procedures for service of process is essential to establish personal jurisdiction. Thus, a default judgment entered against a defendant who was not served with a summons in the manner prescribed by statute is void. Under section 473 (d), the court may set aside a default judgment which is valid on its face, but void, as a matter of law, due to improper service." Ellard v. Conway (2001) 94 Cal.App.4th 540, 544 (internal citations omitted). Defendant was not served. While Plaintiff is correct that there is a presumption of valid service, that presumption is rebutted by Defendant's declaration that she does not fit the description of the individual described in the proof of service. Zorana Breed Decl. ISO Mot., 6. "Here, the proof of service filed by plaintiff included the declaration of a registered process server averring that he personally served defendant . . . Defendant declared that he was not served. Though the trial court was not required to accept this self-serving evidence contradicting the process server's declaration, the proof of service on its face indicates that the process server did not comply with the rules governing service. It . . . describes defendant as an Asian with black hair, a description that does not fit defendant. The proof of service was therefore untruthful." Am. Express Centurion Bank v. Zara (2011) 199 Cal.App.4th 383, 390. As a result of the COVID-19 emergency, all attorneys and parties are required to appear remotely. Hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/CEK)

rulings.Case Title: THE RAJ AND SONAL ABHYANKER FAMILY TRUST ET AL VS. MASON BLAKE ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Order To Show Cause (Added Per Order Filed 7/28/20)(Part 2 Of 2)(For Tentative Ruling Only)
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, August 18, 2020, Line 10, Order To Show Cause (Added Per Order Filed 7/28/20)(Part 2 Of 2)(For Tentative Ruling Only) Third, Plaintiff's claim that the preliminary injunction is barred by the doctrine of unclean hands because he fails to support his assertion that UpCounsel purportedly engaged in "plagiarism." Neither Plaintiff's assertion in his declaration of his unsupported belief that such plagiarism occurred nor hearsay assertions by "third-party Twitter users" constitutes admissible evidence. Fourth and finally, Plaintiff's contention that he "is not the right party to be enjoined" is meritless. He claims that Infovium is not his agent and that he has no control over it, but he admits that he retained it and has already notified it to refrain from selling, posting, publishing, or disclosing any of Defendant's copyrighted content and data. Further, Plaintiff's inconsistent statements about his past conduct give the Court no confidence that he is not, in fact, directly or indirectly complicit in threatened copyright infringement. As a result of the COVID-19 emergency, all attorneys and parties are required to appear remotely. Hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. (end of 2 of 2) =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: THE RAJ AND SONAL ABHYANKER FAMILY TRUST ET AL VS. MASON BLAKE ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Order To Show Cause (Added Per Order Filed 7/28/20)
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, August 18, 2020, Line 10, Order To Show Cause (Added Per Order Filed 7/28/20). (part 1 of 2) The hearing will be held at 1:30 p.m. by videoconference (Zoom). Defendants' application for a preliminary injunction restraining and enjoining Plaintiff Ray Abhyanker, Trustee of the Raj and Sonal Abhyanker Family Trust, and counsel for Plaintiffs through his law firm, LegalForce RAPC Worldwide P.C., and his or its beneficiaries, trustees, agents, employees, and those persons or entities in active concert or participation with any of them, from any further copyright infringement, disclosure, or publication of UpCounsel's copyrighted content or data, and from any interference with Defendants' contract with NewCo, is granted. Plaintiff does not dispute Defendants' showing that he hired Infovium Web Scraping, a web-scraping company located in India, to scrape UpCounsel's copyrighted web content; that he threatened to publish that content, then claimed to have "changed his mind"; and then, after asserting that he had asked Infovium to place a discovery hold on the content, first asserted that the content had been automatically deleted, and then claimed that Infovium "is selling/sold the scraped content." Further, at the hearing on Defendants' TRO, Plaintiff stated, "This content is the value of the company. We are holding it in custody for the remainder of this lawsuit. . . . We are going to be holding it because we have an active litigation." Yet in his declaration, he now asserts, "I do not believe that I possess any copyrighted content and data owned by Defendant, and hence [t]here is nothing to return to Defendant's attorneys." To put it plainly, Plaintiff's inconsistent and self-contradictory assertions lack any credibility. Plaintiff's various responses lack merit. First, Plaintiff's claim that UpCounsel, Inc. lacks standing because it does not own the copyrighted material in question is baseless. By the terms of the March 11, 2020 asset purchase agreement by which UpCounsel, Inc. sold substantially all of its assets to NewCo, excluded from that transfer were "all rights to any action, suit or claim of any nature available to or being pursued by Seller, which arising by counterclaim or otherwise." Thus, Defendants retained the rights to the copyright infringement claims they assert here, together with other claims arising from their first priority lien and security interest in the transferred assets, which include the intellectual property. Second, Plaintiff claims that the preliminary injunction is barred by a February 15, 2019 settlement agreement in federal court between LegalForce and UpCounsel. But the release in that settlement agreement did not purpose to release unknown future that arose after the agreement was entered into. Plaintiff's conduct that gave rise to Defendants' application occurred in March 2020, more than one year after the settlement agreement was entered into. It is Plaintiff's position, not Defendants', that is frivolous.(end of part 1 of 2) =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: JOHN FRIED VS. WOOKEY SEARCH TECHNOLOGIES CORPORATION, ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, August 18, 2020, Line 12, 2-DEFENDANT WOOKEY SEARCH TECHNOLOGIES CORPORATION,, MARK GUSTAVSON's MOTION TO STRIKE 1ST Amended COMPLAINT. Defendants Wookey and Gustavson's motion to strike the Amended Complaint is denied. The fraud, concealment, and breach of fiduciary duty claims support the prayer for punitive damages. The conspiracy allegations are proper as the Amended Complaint presently has Doe defendants. Lastly, plaintiff may seek emotional distress damages on the fraud claims. Branch v. Homefed Bank (1992) 6 Cal.App.4th 793, 799 ["We agree that in cases of intentional misrepresentation recovery for emotional distress need not be accompanied by physical injury."]; Civil Code sections 1709, 3333. A response to the amended complaint (not including the negligence claim as to which the demurrer is sustained) must be filed and served not later than August 31, 2020. As a result of the COVID-19 emergency, all attorneys and parties are required to appear remotely. Hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which states that the motion was not opposed and must email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/CEK)

rulings.Case Title: JOHN FRIED VS. WOOKEY SEARCH TECHNOLOGIES CORPORATION, ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, August 18, 2020, Line 12, 1-DEFENDANT WOOKEY SEARCH TECHNOLOGIES CORPORATION,, MARK GUSTAVSON's DEMURRER to 1ST Amended COMPLAINT. The demurrers to the fraud, concealment, and breach of fiduciary duty causes of action are overruled. "Even under the strict rules of common law pleading, one of the canons was that less particularity is required when the facts lie more in the knowledge of the opposite party ...." Turner v. Milstein (1951) 103 Cal.App.2d 651, 658. Gustavson knows whether he harbored a fraudulent intent. The partnership and minority shareholder allegations support the existence of a fiduciary relationship. See Stephenson v. Dreyer (1997) 16 Cal.4th 1167, 1178 [majority shareholder owes fiduciary duty to minority shareholder]; Amended Complaint 15:9-10. The demurrer to the negligent misrepresentation cause of action is overruled. The Amended Complaint pleads misrepresentations of past or existing facts. Amended Complaint 4:13 [Plaintiff and Gustavson "were partners"]; 5:11 ["WOOKEY had secured at least $4 million in funds"]; 5:16 ["corporate funds were available"]. The demurrer to the negligence cause of action is sustained without leave to amend. The claim is either redundant of the negligent misrepresentation claim and therefore superfluous, or an improper claim for negligent breach of contract. See North American Chemical Co. v. Superior Court (1997) 59 Cal.App.4th 764, 774. The demurrer to the wrongful discharge in violation of public policy cause of action is overruled. Plaintiff alleges that he was discharged for exercising his rights under Labor Code sections 204 and 1194 by seeking his wages. Gustavson demurrers to causes of action 5, 7, 9, 10, and 11 on the basis that the claims could only be asserted against employer Wookey. These demurrers are overruled. Paragraph 59 of the Amended Complaint alleges that "MARK GUSTAVSON individually and on behalf of WOOKEY and Plaintiff JON FRIED entered into an oral contract." The court must take this allegation as true and there is a factual issue regarding the parties to the contract and the identity of the plaintiff's employer. Further, Gustavson may be liable on the Labor Code wage claims under Labor Code section 558.1(a). As a result of the COVID-19 emergency, all attorneys and parties are required to appear remotely. Hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which states that the motion was not opposed and must email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/CEK)
